mnemonics.title: Three Pillars of Critical Section
mnemonics.content: Remember: MPB - Mutual exclusion, Progress, Bounded waiting. Think of 'My Pet Bear' to remember the three essential requirements that must be satisfied.

mnemonics.title: Semaphore Operations
mnemonics.content: Wait decreases (P for 'Passeer' Dutch for 'pass' - you pass through by decrementing), Signal increases (V for 'Verhogen' Dutch for 'increase'). P→-, V→+.

mnemonics.title: Race Condition Detection
mnemonics.content: RACE: Race condition happens when - A (Access to shared data), C (Concurrent execution), E (Execution order affects outcome). Look for: multiple processes, shared variables, non-atomic operations.

acronyms.usage: The fundamental synchronization problem where we must design a protocol that allows processes to safely access shared resources.
The portion of code where shared resources are accessed and requires protection from concurrent access.
The part of the process code outside the critical section where shared resources are not accessed.

acronyms.expansion: Peterson's Algorithm Elements - Process, Entry, Turn, Flag
acronyms.usage: The key elements in Peterson's solution: process ID, flag array indicating desire to enter, and turn variable for tie-breaking.
Hardware instruction that atomically tests and sets a lock variable, enabling implementation of spinlocks.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a semaphore as a counter at a theater entrance. Initial value = number of seats. Each 'wait' means a person takes a seat (decrement). When counter = 0, new arrivals must wait in queue. Each 'signal' means someone leaves (increment), and one waiting person can enter.
Draw a circular diagram with Entry Section → Critical Section → Exit Section → Remainder Section. Show how processes flow through these states and where synchronization happens (at entry and exit).

Show two parallel tracks (processes) merging to a single resource (variable). Without synchronization, both try to use the track simultaneously causing a 'crash' (inconsistent data). With proper locking, they take turns.

analogies.analogy: Think of two people trying to update the same Google Doc simultaneously without Google detecting conflicts - they might overwrite each other's changes, and only one person's changes will be saved. This is exactly what happens in a race condition.
A single toilet in a bathroom - only one person can use it at a time. The lock on the door provides mutual exclusion. This is the simplest real-world example of the critical section problem.

Semaphore is like a hotel key card system - multiple room keys exist (counting), any staff can give out keys to any room. Mutex is like your house key - only you have it, and only you can unlock your own door.
Waiting for a parking spot by circling the block (burning gas/CPU) vs getting a call when a spot opens (being blocked). Spinlocks are useful when spots free up quickly (short critical sections).
